feed.title: Get Lost. Then find your way back. - Trail Running
feed.subtitle: Explore everything

entry.title: The 2019 Yakima Skyline 50k
entry.summary: I normally don't write up trail reports. Normally I run, I have a good time, I finish, and that's about it. The stories are always about some personal victory or distance goal. This is not one of those stories. This is a story of trying to quit and being convinced …
entry.content:
I normally don't write up trail reports. Normally I run, I have a good time, I finish, and that's about it. The stories are always about some personal victory or distance goal. This is not one of those stories. This is a story of trying to quit and being convinced to push on.

At this point in my trail running career I'm starting to see a lot of familiar faces. Rolling into camp the night before the 2019 Yakima Skyline 50k, I run into a few guys from the climbing gym. Not 5 minutes later some old trail running partners show up with beer. A text buzzes my phone notifying me that some other runners will be late. One of the race directors chats up our merry little band. Yup, we're excited and no one has really picked up the mileage since the Orcas Island races. Someone asks me about my Ragnar race from the previous weekend and how the lack of sleep has affected my training. At this point I realize that consecutive race weekends are probably not a great idea. Mistake number 1.

At this point I'm three beers in and thoroughly enjoying myself. Mistake number 2.

The following morning I'm changing into racing clothes, filling water bladders and making coffee. Other runners are milling about, getting ready and making morning chat. Feeling a little groggy, I get to the starting line a bit early. Considering the starting line is all of a couple hundred feet from camp, that's saying something. The final decision on racing clothes landed on 8" shorts, calf sleeves and a tank top. This leaves me pretty exposed to the elements, but it's supposed to be warm. I'll take exposure to baking in too many layers any day.

For shoes I decide against my road shoes and my oft-used S-Labs. Instead I go for a pair that I've only used a handful of times before. Spoiler alert, they're in the trash now. These shoes had given me problems during the Deception Pass 50k, but I chalked that up to poor training and an excess of training. I was wrong. Mistake number 3.

We off at a fairly slow pace leading up to the first main climb. What everyone else knows that I don't (I signed up for this without reading anything. Mistake number 4) is that as soon as we start the first 2000ft climb, the group turns into a mile-long conga line of climbing on single track. I get locked into a crew of chatters spending the climb wise-cracking at each other and myself. We make it to the top together and cruise across the ridgeline to the first aid/water station. By now we're about 5ish miles in.

With a slight amount of shock, I come to the realization that I've only had a couple sips of water. Mistake number 5. A little over 2 miles of downhill later to aid station number 2 and this mistake repeats itself. Alright, Kevin, let's remember to stay hydrated. I know you've been training for races with less water, but this is supposed to make you more economical, not more negligent.

We come to the next big uphill and the rest of the group beats me to the top. I'm starting to slow down and really feel these hills. By the time I get to the next downhill I lose sight of the rest of the group and I start to walk a little. The footing is on loose talus and scree and making running difficult. Immediately I become envious of runners with trek poles. About 3 miles from the next aid station my water runs out. I'm in trouble.

Rolling into the next aid station at the halfway point, I'm in rough condition. I ask about whether or not I should drop. I think I should. They think otherwise. I eat a ton of salty things and drink a ton. Mistake number 6. Somehow, I forgot my salt at home, and they didn't have any either. My feet are incredibly sore and I already hate my shoes, but I pull myself together and trot out of the aid station.

If I keep the same pace back, I should get in in just over 6 hours total. That's not going to happen.

About halfway up hill 3 I'm starting to hurt. My feet are killing me. The loose talus is bruising the insoles and the supposedly grippy rubber on my shoes is doing nothing. People start passing me. I slip from being in the top 50 to way back in the pack. A headache is starting to form, and suddenly I'm nauseous, but nothing is coming up.
